gaius_norbanus_convo_0_n01.npcDialogue: See these men? Half haven't drawn pay in six months. The other half got land grants written on papyrus that turns out to be worth exactly the papyrus. Sit down if you want. I'm finishing this cup either way.

responses.playerDialogue: I'll sit. You seem like the only honest man in the room — and that makes you either brave or drunk.
Those men look like they've earned better than bad wine and empty promises. What happened to their land?
Unpaid veterans drinking together near the Campus Martius. This isn't drowning sorrows — someone called this muster.
If they have legitimate grievances, there are tribunals for land disputes. The senate has procedures.

fallbackResponse.playerDialogue: Pour me whatever that is. I'll catch up.

gaius_norbanus_convo_0_n02.npcDialogue: Norbanus. Praetor on the rolls, soldier in practice. Here's the short version. Marius promised these men land after the Cimbri — good farmland, senate-confirmed. Ten years on, they're sharecropping fields owned by senators who've never gripped anything sharper than a stylus. That's not politics, friend. That's robbery with a clerk's seal on it.

options.playerDialogue: They bled for that land. A promise from the Republic should mean something, or the Republic means nothing.
I watched one of them drill outside — force of habit, I suppose. Soldiers like that don't just evaporate because you cheat them.
If the grants were confirmed by senatorial decree, there must be a legal path. Someone could bring a case.

gaius_norbanus_convo_0_n03.npcDialogue: Simple as mud. Senate votes the land. Commissioners ride out to survey it. Commissioners ride back and say — oh, terribly sorry — the land's occupied by citizens who can't be displaced. Hercules' balls, those citizens all happen to be clients of the same four senators who voted against the grants. Veterans get nothing. Marius fumes. Nobody gives a damn about angry old generals — until the barbarians come back.

options.playerDialogue: Trained killers with nothing to lose and empty bellies. The senate's building itself a very elegant funeral pyre.
The Italian allies fought those same campaigns and got even less. At least your veterans can vote.
If the grants were confirmed by senatorial decree, there must be a legal path. Someone could bring a case.

gaius_norbanus_convo_0_n04.npcDialogue: Procedures. By the gods. I've watched these men file petitions for a decade. You know what a petition gets you? A clerk who loses it, then a second clerk who finds it and loses it better. The whole system's designed to grind you down until you stop asking. But sure — procedures.

options.playerDialogue: Fine. If the legal road's a dead end, tell me what road you think isn't.

gaius_norbanus_convo_0_n05.npcDialogue: Right. So understand what Marius did about it. He threw the legions open to the head-count — men without a pot to piss in. Armed them from the treasury. Turned beggars into the finest heavy infantry the world has ever seen. Brilliant, militarily. But here's the hook — those soldiers owe Rome nothing, because Rome never gave them anything. Their general did. So now every army is a private army, and the senate sits there pretending it isn't. That's the world we drink in.

options.playerDialogue: The man who commands the most legions owns the Republic. Marius understands that arithmetic — he invented it.
Someone has to fix the machinery before it flies apart. Is that what the populares think they're doing?

gaius_norbanus_convo_0_n06.npcDialogue: Now you're talking. The Italians fought beside us — bled beside us, same mud, same fear. And they can't even cast a ballot. A Roman veteran at least has citizenship to his name. An Italian veteran has nothing but a gladius and the memory of friends who died for a Republic that pretends he doesn't exist. The Social War started because we were too gods-damned stupid to share a vote.

options.playerDialogue: Veterans and Italians want the same thing — land, recognition. Has anyone tried yoking those two oxen together?
Talk like that will put you on the optimates' list of people to ruin. You know that.

gaius_norbanus_convo_0_n07.npcDialogue: I respect a man who believes in the law. Truly. But listen — these veterans believed in it too. They marched under the eagle because the law said serve and you'll be repaid. The law broke its word first. So when they stop believing, don't wag your finger at them. Wag it at the men who taught them the law is a liar's game.

options.playerDialogue: That's a fair hit. So what do you think should actually be done?

gaius_norbanus_convo_0_n08.npcDialogue: You think straight and you don't flinch from the answer. I like that. There's a tribune called Sulpicius — sharp as a Gallic blade, willing to push laws the senate would rather swallow poison than pass. I'll tell him you're worth a conversation. Come find me again when the war starts heating up. It will.

gaius_norbanus_convo_0_n09.npcDialogue: You're not one of us — not yet. But you sat down and you listened, which is more than most men from your end of the hill bother doing. Keep your ears open, Crispus. The veterans' patience has an edge, and it's getting shorter every month.

gaius_norbanus_convo_0_n10.npcDialogue: Off you go, then. Back up the hill to your marble portico. Tell your people the veterans send their regards — they'll know what it means, even if you don't.

deltas.reason: Norbanus vouches for you to the Marian network
Showed genuine concern for the veterans' plight
Norbanus considers you approachable but uncommitted
The veterans see you as another senate mouthpiece

effects.reason: Marian path slowed — veterans won't vouch for you
